title: EXAMINATION: LIP DEW (W01849635)
text:
OPERATIONS DEPARTMENT
M123
Ex officio refusal of protection (Article 7, Article 42(2)
Alicante, 19/03/2026
ACCOMPLY
Via Soperga, 2
I-20127 Milano
ITALIA
Your reference: IA00003538510_01
International registration No: 1849635
Trade Mark: LIP DEW
Name of the holder: VIEVE LIMITED
17c Townhead Street,
STRATHAVEN
Lanarkshire ML10 6AB
United Kingdom
I. Summary of the facts
On 12/05/2025 the Office issued a Notification of ex officio provisional refusal of protection
pursuant to Article 7(1)(b) and (c) and Article 7(2) EUTMR because it found that the trade
mark applied for is descriptive and devoid of any distinctive character.
The goods for which the provisional refusal was issued were:
Class 3 Colour cosmetics.
The grounds for refusal were based on the following main findings:
The relevant English-speaking consumer would understand the sign as having the following
meaning: a cosmetic product or effect designed to give the lips a hydrated, glossy, and fresh
look.
The abovementioned meanings of the words ‘LIP DEW’, of which the trade mark consists,
are supported by the following dictionary references.
LIP “either of the two fleshy folds surrounding the mouth, playing an important
role in the production of speech sounds, retaining food in the mouth, etc.”
(information extracted from Collins English Dictionary on 08/05/2025 at
https://www.collinsdictionary.com/dictionary/english/lip).
DEW “small drops of moisture” (information extracted from Collins English
Avenida de Europa, 4 • E - 03008 • Alicante, Spain
Tel. +34 965139100 • www.euipo.europa.eu

Page 2 of 9
Dictionary on 08/05/2025 at
https://www.collinsdictionary.com/dictionary/english/dew).
The relevant content of these links was reproduced in the Notification of ex officio provisional
refusal of protection.
The relevant consumers would perceive the sign as providing information that goods are
high-shine lip glosses. Therefore, the sign describes the kind of the goods.
Given that the sign has a clear descriptive meaning, it is also devoid of any distinctive
character and therefore ineligible for registration under Article 7(1)(b) EUTMR. This means
that it is incapable of performing the essential function of a trade mark, which is to
distinguish the goods or services of one undertaking from those of other undertakings.
In addition, the holder was requested to appoint a representative entitled to represent third
parties before the Office, pursuant to Articles 119(2) and 120(1) EUTMR.
The holder submitted its observations on 11/07/2025, which included a reservation of the
right to claim acquired distinctiveness under Article 7(3) EUTMR. In response to this
reservation, on 24/09/2025 the Office sent Communication regarding Article 7(3) EUTMR
and Article 2(2) EUTMIR and invited the holder to submit the corresponding clarification on
whether it indeed claims that the trade mark applied for has acquired distinctiveness through
use pursuant to Article 7(3) EUTMR and on the nature of the claim (principal or subsidiary)
within two months of notification of this communication. In addition, if the claim was intended
to be a principal one, evidence of acquired distinctiveness must be submitted within the
same time limit. The Office informed [the holder] that if it does not reply to this
communication or if the reply submitted does not clarify the nature of the claim, the Office
will consider that no claim of acquired distinctiveness through use pursuant to Article 7(3)
EUTMR has been made and will decide only on the inherent distinctiveness of the trade
mark applied for.
The holder failed to submit any response or evidence.
II. Summary of the holder’s arguments
The holder submitted its observations on 11/07/2025, which may be summarised as follows:
The sign applied for is distinctive and not descriptive of the goods for which protection is
sought. The mark is the unusual and memorable linguistic pairing of "LIP" and "DEW"; the
figurative, poetic quality of the expression; and the mark's phonetic and conceptual
impression. "LIP DEW," when assessed as a complete sign, constitutes a distinctive
neologism that is not commonly employed in trade to describe cosmetic products. The
juxtaposition of "LIP" and "DEW" is unexpected and requires intellectual effort to connect to
any purported characteristics of the goods, placing the mark in the category of suggestive
rather than descriptive marks. The metaphorical use of "DEW" in relation to cosmetics,
arguing that its application to cosmetic products is figurative — evoking freshness,
naturalness and subtle moisture — rather than providing a direct description of the product's
function or characteristics.
The average consumer would perceive "LIP DEW" as a brand identifier that suggests certain
qualities through metaphorical language, rather than as a direct description of product
characteristics. The Office's reliance on dictionary definitions is insufficient to establish
descriptiveness without demonstrating that consumers actually perceive the mark as
descriptive in the specific commercial context. The Office has provided no evidence that "LIP

Page 3 of 9
DEW" is commonly used in the cosmetics trade as a descriptive term, arguing that such
absence of evidence undermines the descriptiveness finding. When consumers encounter
"LIP DEW" on cosmetic products they would perceive it as indicating the source of those
goods rather than merely describing their characteristics.
Numerous alternative descriptive terms — such as "moisturizing," "hydrating," "glossy,"
"shine," and "luminous" — remain freely available to competitors to describe cosmetic
products that provide moisture or gloss to the lips. The registration of the sign applied for
does not compromise the public interest in keeping descriptive terms free, because the
metaphorical and suggestive nature of the mark means it does not occupy descriptive space
that competitors would naturally need.
The Office's own registration practice demonstrates acceptance of similar marks combining
body parts with other terms, and that the current refusal represents an inconsistent
application of the Office's standards. The Office has previously recognised that metaphorical
usage and unusual combinations can create registrable marks even when individual
components might have descriptive meanings.
The holder makes a reservation of the right to demonstrate acquired distinctiveness through
use under Article 7(3) EUTMR if required and offers to restrict the specification to specific
goods within Class 3 that do not raise the descriptiveness concerns identified by the Office,
should the Office maintain its position.
III. Reasons
Pursuant to Article 94 EUTMR, it is up to the Office to take a decision based on reasons or
evidence on which the holder has had an opportunity to present its comments.
After giving due consideration to the holder's arguments, the Office has decided to maintain
the grounds for refusal.
Under Article 7(1)(c) EUTMR, ‘trade marks which consist exclusively of signs or indications
which may serve, in trade, to designate the kind, quality, quantity, intended purpose, value,
geographical origin or the time of production of the goods or of rendering of the service, or
other characteristics of the goods or service’ are not to be registered.
It is settled case-law that each of the grounds for refusal to register listed in Article 7(1)
EUTMR is independent and requires separate examination. Moreover, it is appropriate to
interpret those grounds for refusal in the light of the general interest underlying each of them.
The general interest to be taken into consideration must reflect different considerations
according to the ground for refusal in question (16/09/2004, C-329/02 P, SAT.2,
EU:C:2004:532, § 25).
By prohibiting the registration as European Union trade marks of the signs and indications to
which it refers, Article 7(1)(c) EUTMR
pursues an aim which is in the public interest, namely that descriptive signs or
indications relating to the characteristics of goods or services in respect of which
registration is sought may be freely used by all. That provision accordingly
prevents such signs and indications from being reserved to one undertaking
alone because they have been registered as trade marks.
(23/10/2003, C-191/01 P, Doublemint, EU:C:2003:579, § 31).
The signs and indications referred to in Article 7(1)(c) [EUTMR] are those which may serve

Page 4 of 9
in normal usage from the point of view of the target public to designate, either directly or by
reference to one of their essential characteristics, the goods or service in respect of which
registration is sought’ (26/11/2003, T-222/02, ROBOTUNITS, EU:T:2003:315, § 34).
As the holder failed to submit any response or evidence within the prescribed time limit, and
in accordance with the Office's communication of 24/09/2025, the Office considers that no
claim of acquired distinctiveness through use pursuant to Article 7(3) EUTMR has been
made. The Office therefore decides solely on the basis of the inherent distinctiveness of the
trade mark applied for.
The holder conditionally offered to restrict the specification to goods within Class 3 that do
not raise descriptiveness concerns. However, this conditional offer does not constitute a
formal restriction of the goods. The Office notes that a formal, unconditional restriction must
be submitted in accordance with the applicable procedural rules. Furthermore, the Office
observes that any such restriction would need to be assessed on its merits to determine
whether the descriptiveness objection would be resolved in respect of the remaining goods.
As no formal restriction was submitted, the Office proceeds on the basis of the specification
as filed.
Regarding the holder’s arguments
The mark applied for comprises two verbal elements: ‘LIP’ and ‘DEW’.
In view of the definitions and analysis provided on 12/05/2025, the sign as a whole is
descriptive for the English-speaking public. The English-speaking part of the European
Union includes not only countries where English is an official language, such as Ireland and
Malta, but also those where, at the very least, English is widely understood. This includes
Denmark, Cyprus, the Netherlands, Finland and Sweden (20/01/2021, T-253/20, It’s like milk
but made for humans, EU:T:2021:21, § 35; 09/12/2010, T-307/09, NATURALLY ACTIVE,
EU:T:2010:509, § 26-27).
The goods to which an objection has been raised target the general public and
professionals. Colour cosmetics are everyday consumer goods purchased regularly and at
relatively modest prices. When acquiring such goods, the average consumer's level of
attention is not particularly high, and consumers are unlikely to engage in detailed analysis
of a mark's component words. Therefore, the general public’s degree of attention varies
between average and that of a reasonably well-informed consumer, and the professional
consumer’s level of attention is high.
However, the fact that part of the relevant public is a specialist one, whose degree of
attention is higher than average, cannot decisively influence the legal criteria used to assess
the distinctive character of a sign. The Court of Justice has stated that ‘it does not
necessarily follow that a weaker distinctive character of a sign is sufficient where the relevant
public is specialist’ (12/07/2012, C 311/11 P, Wir machen das Besondere einfach,
EU:C:2012:460, § 48).
There is nothing unusual about the sign’s structure. It has no unusual variation in syntax or
complex meaning, and nothing that would require an interpretative effort by the relevant
public to understand it, or that would initiate a cognitive process in the minds of consumers.
This is because the sign’s meaning is clear. Furthermore, it cannot be perceived as
imaginative, vague, odd, allusive, suggestive, fanciful, ambiguous, surprising, striking,
arbitrary or unexpected. Furthermore, the holder failed to submit any substantiated
information or evidence to show that the relevant public would understand the sign applied
for differently or would not understand it.

Page 5 of 9
The holder's argument that 'DEW' is used metaphorically and requires intellectual effort to
connect to product characteristics cannot be accepted. When encountered in the context of
lip cosmetics, the word 'DEW' immediately and directly evokes moisture, glossiness and a
fresh, hydrated appearance — qualities that are directly descriptive of the relevant goods.
The connection between 'DEW' and these product characteristics does not require any
imaginative step on the part of the consumer. The mere fact that 'DEW' has a primary
meaning relating to atmospheric moisture does not render its application to cosmetics
metaphorical in a legally relevant sense; rather, it constitutes a straightforward transposition
of the concept of moisture to the cosmetic context.
Furthermore, the average consumer tends not to analyse trade marks in depth. A trade mark
must, therefore, enable average consumers of the goods in question, who are reasonably
well informed and reasonably observant and circumspect, to distinguish the goods
concerned from those of other undertakings without conducting an analytical or comparative
examination and without paying particular attention (12/02/2004, C 218/01, Perwoll,
EU:C:2004:88, § 53; 12/01/2006, C 173/04 P, Standbeutel, EU:C:2006:20, § 29).
Nevertheless, the Office duly explained the meaning of the mark in the objection letter and
supported its findings with dictionary definitions of the sign’s components, which reflect how
the mark will be understood in the relevant market.
The relevant consumers would perceive the sign as providing information that goods are
high-shine lip glosses. Therefore, the sign describes the kind of the goods.
For a trade mark to be refused registration under Article 7(1)(c) EUTMR,
it is not necessary that the signs and indications composing the mark that are
referred to in that Article actually be in use at the time of the application for
registration in a way that is descriptive of goods or services such as those in
relation to which the application is filed, or of characteristics of those goods or
services. It is sufficient, as the wording of that provision itself indicates, that such
signs and indications could be used for such purposes. A sign must therefore
be refused registration under that provision if at least one of its possible
meanings designates a characteristic of the goods or services concerned.
(23/10/2003, C-191/01 P, Doublemint, EU:C:2003:579, § 32, emphasis added).
Moreover,
[i]t is… irrelevant whether the characteristics of the goods or services which may
be the subject of the description are commercially essential or merely ancillary.
The wording of Article 7(1)(c) EUTMR does not draw any distinction by reference
to the characteristics which may be designated by the signs or indications of
which the mark consists. In fact, in the light of the public interest underlying the
provision, any undertaking must be able freely to use such signs and indications
to describe any characteristic whatsoever of its own goods, irrespective of how
significant the characteristic may be commercially.
(12/02/2004, C-363/99, Postkantoor, EU:C:2004:86, § 102).
Since the trade mark at issue is made up of several components, for the purposes of
assessing its distinctive character it must be considered as a whole. However, this is not
incompatible with an examination of each of the mark’s individual components in turn
(19/09/2001, T-118/00, Tabs (3D), EU:T:2001:226, § 59).

Page 6 of 9
For a trade mark that consists of a neologism, or a word produced by a combination of
elements, to be regarded as descriptive within the meaning of Article 7(1)(c) EUTMR, ‘it is
not sufficient that each of its components may be found to be descriptive. The word or
neologism itself must be found to be so’ (12/01/2005, T-367/02- T-369/02, SnTEM, SnPUR
& SnMIX, EU:T:2005:3, § 31).
However,
[a] trade mark consisting of a neologism or a word composed of elements each
of which is descriptive of characteristics of the goods or services in respect of
which registration is sought is itself descriptive of the characteristics of those
goods or services for the purposes of Article 7(1)(c) EUTMR, unless there is a
perceptible difference between the neologism or the word and the mere sum of
its parts: that assumes that, because of the unusual nature of the combination in
relation to the goods or services, the neologism or word creates an impression
which is sufficiently far removed from that produced by the mere combination of
meanings lent by the elements of which it is composed, with the result that the
word is more than the sum of its parts…
(12/01/2005, T-367/02 - T-369/02, SnTEM, SnPUR & SnMIX, EU:T:2005:3, § 32).
In the same sense, an analysis of the term in question, in the light of the relevant lexical and
grammatical rules, is also useful (30/11/2004, T-173/03, NURSERYROOM, EU:T:2004:347,
§ 21).
It follows from the abovementioned dictionary definitions provided by the Office that the
verbal elements ‘LIP’ and ‘DEW’ are not completely unrelated concepts, since the former
qualifies the latter. The combination of verbal elements follows a straightforward grammatical
structure in which 'LIP' functions as a qualifier identifying the product type and area of
application, and 'DEW' describes the finish or effect. This structure is entirely conventional
and produces no impression that is surprising, incongruous, or sufficiently removed from the
sum of its parts to render the combination distinctive. Bearing in mind these dictionary
definitions, the relevant consumer will have no difficulty understanding the sign as a whole to
mean that the goods are lip cosmetics with a dewy, moist finish. There is no perceptible
difference between the combination and the sum of its descriptive components.
The fact that the sign or the combination applied for is not commonly used does not
necessarily lead to the finding that it is inherently distinctive in relation to the goods in
question. Under Article 7(1)(c) EUTMR, ‘trade marks which consist exclusively of signs or
indications which may serve, in trade, to designate the kind, quality, quantity, intended
purpose, value, geographical origin or the time of production of the goods or of rendering of
the service, or other characteristics of the goods or service’ are not to be registered.
The public interest underlying Article 7(1)(c) EUTMR is that exclusive rights should not exist
for purely descriptive terms that other traders might wish to use as well. However, the Office
does not need to prove that there is already a descriptive use by the holder or its
competitors.
The holder argues that third parties, and more particularly competitors, do not need to use
the sign at issue to designate the goods to which the application relates. However,
Article 3(1)(c) of Directive 89/104, which corresponds to Article 7(1)(c) EUTMR, applies
irrespective of whether there is a real, current or serious need to leave a sign or indication
free (27/02/2002, T-106/00, Streamserve, EU:T:2002:43, § 39).

Page 7 of 9
The sign has a clear descriptive meaning. Therefore, it is also devoid of any distinctive
character and may be objected to under Article 7(1)(b) EUTMR, as it is incapable of
performing the essential function of a trade mark, which is to distinguish the goods of one
undertaking from those of other undertakings.
The informative message of the sign applied for is intended to persuade potential customers
to choose the goods for which protection is sought. The holder’s sign cannot be required to
display imaginativeness, or even ‘conceptual tension that would create surprise and so make
a striking impression’ so as to have a minimal level of distinctiveness. However, the meaning
of the sign applied for is directly descriptive of a characteristic of the relevant goods.
Therefore, it is unlikely to possess any distinctive character because it is highly unlikely to be
immediately perceived as an indication of the commercial origin of the relevant goods.
According to the EUTMR, no undertaking should be given a monopoly right to use banal,
commonplace or everyday terms to promote its commercial activities.
Furthermore, the holder did not submit any substantiated information or evidence to prove
that the sign applied for is original, imaginative and fanciful for the relevant public. This lack
of evidence supports the Office’s findings that the sign cannot be recognised as capable of
performing the essential function of a trade mark.
Moreover, there are no additional verbal or stylistic elements that result in the mark
significantly deviating from being, as a whole, a merely descriptive informational statement.
The holder argues that no other competitors make use of the same combination. However,
the distinctive character of a trade mark is determined on the basis of the fact that the
relevant public can immediately perceive the trade mark as designating the commercial
origin of the goods or services in question. Lack of prior use does not automatically indicate
such a perception (15/09/2005, T-320/03, Live richly, EU:T:2005:325, § 88).
The Court has confirmed that it is not up to the Office to show that other similar signs are
used on the market:
[W]here the Board of Appeal finds that the trade mark sought is devoid of
intrinsic distinctive character, it may base its analysis on facts arising from
practical experience generally acquired from the marketing of general consumer
goods which are likely to be known by anyone and are in particular known by the
consumers of those goods … In such a case, the Board of Appeal is not obliged
to give examples of such practical experience.
(15/03/2006, T-129/04, Plastikflaschenform, EU:T:2006:84, § 19).
It is on the basis of such acquired experience that the Office submits that the relevant
consumers would perceive the sign applied for as non-distinctive and not as the trade mark
of a particular proprietor. Since, despite the Office’s analysis based on such experience, the
holder claims that the trade mark applied for is distinctive, it is up to the holder to provide
specific and substantiated information to show that the trade mark applied for has distinctive
character, either intrinsically or acquired through use; it is much better placed to do so, given
its thorough knowledge of the market (05/03/2003, T-194/01, Soap device, EU:T:2003:53,
§ 48).
The holder has not provided any specific and substantiated information showing that the
trade mark applied for has distinctive character in the relevant market sector that could
disprove the Office’s analysis, which is based on facts arising from practical experience
generally acquired from the marketing of the goods concerned.

Page 8 of 9
The holder referred to other trade marks containing elements with descriptive connotations,
arguing that marks combining body parts with other terms have been accepted where the
overall impression creates a distinctive identifier. However, the holder cannot successfully
rely on the registered trademarks. Every trade mark is assessed on its own merits and the
final assessment is based on specific grounds in each case. The circumstances that led to
the possible future acceptance of other marks cannot impact the objections in this case; nor
are they a valid argument to overcome the objection of the descriptiveness and the lack of
distinctiveness of the mark at issue.
Even if the similar marks were to be registered or accepted by the Office, more favourable
Office decisions in allegedly similar cases do not entitle the mark in question to equal
treatment that may lead to registration. Although a less restrictive approach may have
prevailed for other marks, this does not amount to a violation of the principle of non-
discrimination or to a reason for invalidating a decision that, per se, appears to be
reasonable and to conform to the EUTMR, as interpreted by the EU judicature. Observance
of the principle of equal treatment must be reconciled with observance of the principle of
legality (27/02/2002, T-106/00, STREAMSERVE, EU:T:2002:43; 02/05/2012, T-435/11,
UniversalPHOLED, EU:T:2012:210).
Moreover, according to settled case-law, ‘decisions concerning registration of a mark as a
European Union trade mark… are adopted in the exercise of circumscribed powers and are
not a matter of discretion’. Accordingly, the registrability of a mark as a European Union
trade mark must be assessed solely on the basis of the EUTMR, as interpreted by the EU
judicature, and not on the basis of previous Office practice (15/09/2005, C-37/03 P, BioID,
EU:C:2005:547, § 47; 09/10/2002, T-36/01, Glass pattern, EU:T:2002:245, § 35).
The Office has considered that the mark applied for is similar to other marks that combine
body parts with other terms. However, the Office cannot justify the registration of the sign
based on the above-mentioned, for the reasons explained above.
IV. Conclusion
For the abovementioned reasons, and pursuant to Article 7(1)(b) and (c) and Article 7(2)
EUTMR, protection of the international registration No 1849635 is refused for the European
Union.
According to Article 67 EUTMR, you have a right to appeal against this decision. According
to Article 68 EUTMR, notice of appeal must be filed in writing at the Office within two months
of the date of notification of this decision. It must be filed in the language of the proceedings
in which the decision subject to appeal was taken. Furthermore, a written statement of the
grounds of appeal must be filed within four months of the same date. The notice of appeal
will be deemed to be filed only when the appeal fee of EUR 720 has been paid.

Page 9 of 9
Kaspars PUBULIS
